coredata.dc:title: p-Block anion compressed d/p band center of bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts for durable aqueous Zn–air batteries

coredata.dc:description: Robust bifunctional electrocatalysts that accelerate the sluggish oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are vital for rechargeable aqueous Zn–air batteries (AZABs). In this study, a three-dimensional foam-like N-doped carbon frameworks (NCFs) catalyst with abundant NCoTe2 active sites (CoTe2@NCFs) was successfully constructed using the carbon dots (CDs)-assisted strategy as the efficient bifunctional ORR/OER catalyst. In situ Raman spectra were used to monitor the formation of Co-OOH, which confirmed the dynamic change in oxygen intermediates on the surface of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst. Both theoretical calculations and experimental results demonstrate that the integrated Te element in the p-block results in a reduction in the difference between d/p band centers (Δε d-p), which effectively enhances the rapid adsorption/desorption capability of *OOH/*OH, thereby improving the ORR/OER performance. Impressively, the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst shows excellent bifunctional oxygen catalytic performance (ΔE = 0.66 V). Moreover, the assembled CoTe2@NCFs-based rechargeable AZABs exhibit a high peak power density of 177.8 mW cm−2, substantial specific capacity of 793.4 mAh g −1, and excellent long-term cycling durability over 1000 cycles. The compressed d/p-band synergistic center provides novel insights into the design of bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts for efficient energy storage and conversion devices.

head.ce:title: p-Block anion compressed d/p band center of bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts for durable aqueous Zn–air batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Robust bifunctional electrocatalysts that accelerate the sluggish oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are vital for rechargeable aqueous Zn–air batteries (AZABs). In this study, a three-dimensional foam-like N-doped carbon frameworks (NCFs) catalyst with abundant NCoTe2 active sites (CoTe2@NCFs) was successfully constructed using the carbon dots (CDs)-assisted strategy as the efficient bifunctional ORR/OER catalyst. In situ Raman spectra were used to monitor the formation of Co-OOH, which confirmed the dynamic change in oxygen intermediates on the surface of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst. Both theoretical calculations and experimental results demonstrate that the integrated Te element in the p-block results in a reduction in the difference between d/p band centers (Δε d-p), which effectively enhances the rapid adsorption/desorption capability of *OOH/*OH, thereby improving the ORR/OER performance. Impressively, the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst shows excellent bifunctional oxygen catalytic performance (ΔE = 0.66 V). Moreover, the assembled CoTe2@NCFs-based rechargeable AZABs exhibit a high peak power density of 177.8 mW cm−2, substantial specific capacity of 793.4 mAh g −1, and excellent long-term cycling durability over 1000 cycles. The compressed d/p-band synergistic center provides novel insights into the design of bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts for efficient energy storage and conversion devices.

A 3D foam-like CoTe2@NCFs catalyst with abundant NCoTe2 active sites was successfully constructed via CDs-assisted as the efficient bifunctional ORR/OER catalyst. Theoretical calculations indicate that the incorporation of Te element in the p-block results in the reduction of d/p band center difference (Δε d-p), thereby significantly enhancing the rapid adsorption/desorption capability of *OOH/*OH and improving the performance of ORR/OER.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To address the severe challenges brought by the burning of conventional fossil fuels and environmental degradation, green and recyclable energy storage and conversion technologies must be urgently developed [1,2]. Among various existing new energy candidates, rechargeable aqueous Zn–air batteries (AZABs) have attracted significant attention due to their cost-efficiency, high theoretical energy density (1086 Wh kg−1), and environmental sustainability [3]. However, the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) during charging and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) during discharging on an air cathode undermine the overall output efficiency of AZABs and pose a significant obstacle to their commercialisation [4,5]. To date, advanced Pt-based and Ru-/Ir-based catalysts have exhibited extremely high ORR and OER activity, respectively [6]. However, their high cost, inherent scarcity, and single catalytic activity limit their practical application [7]. Therefore, it is imperative to design and develop efficient and economical non-precious metal-based bifunctional electrocatalysts.

In recent years, 3d transition metal-based electrocatalysts, such as oxides [8], hydroxides [9], nitrides [10], phosphates [11], carbides [12], and chalcogenides [13,14], have demonstrated remarkable potential for optimising ORR and OER properties because of their high theoretical activity and regulated electronic structure [15]. Among them, the performance of transition metal chalcogenides (TMCs, C = S, Se, and Te) in catalysing the ORR and OER has been significantly enhanced owing to the synergistic effect between the appropriate conduction and valence band boundaries and the electron band structure [16,17]. However, pure TMCs have low conductivity and poor stability and can easily agglomerate and corrode during preparation and catalysis, resulting in reduced catalytic stability. Theoretical calculations and electrochemical experiments have proven that the introduction of B, N, F, P, S, or other heteroatoms into carbon carriers can adjust the electronic structure of adjacent carbon atoms, thereby increasing the p-state density near the sp2 carbon and reducing the work function, finally achieving a significant increase in the catalytic activity of carbon-based materials [18,19]. Moreover, the incorporation of heteroatom-doped carbon carriers with TMCs can effectively mitigate TMCs agglomeration and prevent direct interaction between the active sites and electrolyte, thereby enhancing ORR and OER catalyst stability [20,21]. For instance, Ding et al. fabricated double N-doped carbon frameworks embedded with CoSe2 nanoparticles (NPs) using a simple electrospinning technique and discovered that CoSe2@NC@NCNFs exhibited exceptional catalytic activities for the ORR process [22]. Li et al. designed an Fe/Ni-CoTe@NCFs catalyst with a hierarchical hollow structure using a facile and low-cost hydrothermal strategy. Due to the sufficient internal channels and large specific surface area provided by the hollow nanostructure, Fe/Ni-CoTe@NCFs exhibits advantageous OER catalytic activity (η = 287 mV at 10 mA·cm−2) [23]. Despite significant progress in the design of TMCs catalysts, achieving simultaneous bifunctional ORR and OER catalytic activity remains a challenging task.

The bifunctional ORR/OER performance is not only determined by the catalyst structure, electronic conductivity, number of active sites, and exposure but is also influenced by the local electronic structure [24,25]. Various strategies, including structural engineering [26], surface/interface engineering (anionic/cationic modifications [27,28], atomic defect engineering [29], and heterostructures [30]), have been widely used to regulate the electronic structure of catalyst active sites, thereby effectively optimising their catalytic performance. Li et al. constructed oxygen-atom-modified single-atom catalysts based on a self-assembly strategy for ORR and OER. Theoretical calculations demonstrated that the introduction of oxygen atoms induced charge redistribution around Fe1N4, resulting in the displacement of the d-band center from the Fermi level and an increase in the number of anti-bond-occupied states. Consequently, this optimisation enhanced the adsorption/desorption behaviour of the oxygen-containing intermediates, ultimately accelerating *OOH formation in the ORR and the deprotonation of *OH in the OER [31]. Zhang et al. employed a surface pretreatment strategy to synthesise Co4N@d-NCNW/D composites with efficient ORR and OER performance. Theoretical calculations and experimental results confirmed that the incorporation of pyridinium N effectively shifted the d-band center of Co away from the Fermi level, thereby enhancing the desorption capability of oxygen-containing intermediates (*O and *OOH). This modification facilitated charge transfer between the Co4N NPs and carbon-based carriers, leading to a significant reduction in both the energy barriers and total overpotential for the ORR and OER [32]. Therefore, many studies have focused on the d-band center level of transition metal catalysts to elucidate the catalytic mechanisms of the ORR and OER [33]. However, the electronic structure of the catalyst can also be influenced by the p-band center of the nonmetal atoms, which has been overlooked in previous studies [34]. Consequently, synergistic adjustment of the d/p band center holds promise as a novel strategy for the design of efficient and stable bifunctional catalysts.

Among S, Se, and Te elements, the atomic radii increase in the order of S<Se<Te, whereas their electronegativities decrease as S>Se>Te. In particular, Te possesses a significantly higher conductivity of 2 × 10−2 S m −1 compared to that of S (5 × 10−28 S m −1) and Se (1 × 10−3 S m −1). In this study, based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations, a three-dimensional foam-like CoTe2@NCFs catalyst with abundant NCoTe2 active sites was successfully constructed through the CDs-assisted strategy. Theoretical calculations and electrochemical experiments demonstrate that the introduction of Te can significantly enhance the proximity of the d/p band center (shorten Δε d-p) compared to S and Se, thus boosting the adsorption and desorption capacity of *OH, *OOH, and other oxygen-containing intermediates and ultimately improving the performance of ORR and OER. Furthermore, the in situ Raman spectroscopy confirm that the formation of Co-OOH intermediates is conducive to improving OER kinetics. The prepared CoTe2@NCFs delivers a remarkably low potential difference of 0.66 V between the ORR (E 1/2 = 0.85 V) and OER (E j=10 = 1.51 V). Consequently, rechargeable CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB offers excellent power density (177.8 mW cm−2), specific capacity (793.4 mAh g −1), energy density (990.6 Wh kg−1 Zn), and cycling stability over 1000 cycles. This study elucidates a strategic approach for the design and mechanistic dissection of bifunctional electrocatalysts through d/p band center synergy.

DFT calculations were conducted based on the strong interaction between metal chalcogenides and N-doped carbon frameworks to examine the influence of the p-block elements S, Se, and Te on the intrinsic activity of the catalyst. Three theoretical models including CoS2 NC, CoSe2 NC, and CoTe2 NC were constructed for comparison (Fig. 1
a–c and Fig. S1). The bond length of Co−Te (2.61 Å) in CoTe2 NC was found to be greater than those of Co−S (2.26 Å) in CoS2 NC and Co−Se (2.35 Å) in CoSe2 NC, which can be explained by the atomic radius sequence of Te>Se>S. The energy band structures of CoS2 NC, CoSe2 NC, and CoTe2 NC in these three theoretical models show that they have no band gaps (Fig. S2), indicating their metallic behaviour and electrical conductivity, which are favorable for electron transport [35]. The density of states (DOSs) analysis of these three theoretical models revealed that CoTe2 NC has a denser electron-occupied state on the right side of the Fermi level than CoS2 NC and CoSe2 NC (Fig. 1 d-f and Fig. S3), indicating its superior electronic conductivity and enhanced electron transport capabilities [36]. The high conductivity and carrier density of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst further enhance electron transfer between its surface and the adsorbed intermediates [37]. Fig. 1 g-i shows the differential charge density diagram of CoS2 NC, CoSe2 NC, and CoTe2 NC. It can be found that there is obvious charge accumulation, strong charge exchange, and electron interaction at the interface of CoS2, CoSe2, CoTe2, and NC. This observation suggests that the metal component can effectively disrupt the electrical neutrality of the carbon atoms, thereby potentially optimising the adsorption behaviour of oxygen-containing intermediates and enhancing intrinsic catalytic activity [38]. The higher energy levels of the transition metal d-band electrons play a crucial role in facilitating chemical bonding and breaking interactions with the lower-energy-level p-band electrons of non-metallic elements [39]. The d-band center (ε d) of the metallic component (Co) and the p-band center (ε p) of non-metallic elements (S, Se, and Te) were determined based on the results obtained from the density of states calculation. The ε d of Co (−1.81 eV) in CoTe2 NC exhibited a negative shift compared to that in CoS2 NC (−1.13 eV) and CoSe2 NC (−1.58 eV) (Fig. 1 j), suggesting an enhanced occupation of antibonding orbitals by CoTe2 NC. The adsorption strength and stability of the oxygen intermediates at the catalytically active sites are determined by the extent of electron filling in the antibonding orbital between the metal d electronic state and the oxygen-containing intermediates, as per the d-band theory [40]. This phenomenon implies that CoTe2 NC exhibits an enhanced occupancy of the antibonding orbitals, thereby weakening the binding affinity towards molecular oxygen and facilitating molecular dissociation [41]. Interestingly, the p-band center of the non-metallic anions are regularly distributed, as shown in Fig. 1 k. Specifically, the ε p of CoS2 NC, CoSe2 NC, and CoTe2 NC are −3.21, −2.89, and −2.63 eV, respectively. The p-band center of CoTe2 NC, which is influenced by the largest atomic radius and lowest electronegativity of Te, shifts towards the Fermi level. This upward displacement effectively enhances the generation of valence band electrons, thereby increasing the electron energy and promoting the adsorption and desorption of oxygen intermediates on the active sites [42]. The ε d value for the Co atom and the ε p values for the S, Se, and Te atoms indicate hybridisation of their valence electron orbitals (Fig. 1l). The d/p band center difference (Δε d-p) serves as a significant descriptor for predicting electrocatalytic activity in metal compounds. Considering that the ε Co(d) center of the CoTe2 NC shifts downward relative to the corresponding centers of CoS2 NC and CoSe2 NC, the Δε d-p moves upward in the order of Δε Co(d)-Te(p) < Δε Co(d)-Se(p) < Δε Co(d)-S(p). The synergy of the d/p band centerfacilitates the adsorption and desorption of oxygen-containing intermediates such as *OH and *OOH, potentially improving electrocatalytic performance [43].

Based on the DFT calculations, a CD-assisted strategy was proposed to construct a three-dimensional foam-like CoTe2@NCFs catalyst with abundant NCoTe2 active sites, as shown in Fig. 2
a. A comprehensive description of the fabrication process is provided in Supplementary Information. First, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) enriched with N-rich functional groups were synthesised by incorporating carbamide during the condensation reaction of acetaldehyde and sodium hydroxide at ambient temperature [44]. Ethanol was then used as the medium to incorporate quantitative amounts of cobalt salts and NCDs. Owing to the exceptional solubility and dispersion of the NCDs in ethanol, vigorous stirring yielded a uniformly dispersed brown solution, wherein Co2+ could effectively coordinate with the N-rich functional groups present in the NCDs. The resulting mixture was subjected to rotary evaporation until ethanol was completely removed, yielding a dark brown Co2+/NCD powder. Subsequently, during high-temperature annealing, a substantial amount of gas was generated as certain NCDs decomposed, while some carbon atoms underwent graphitisation [45]. Simultaneously, Co2+ was reduced by carbon to form Co NPs, which aided by the rapid expansion of a substantial amount of gas and the catalytic effect of Co NPs. The zero-dimensional NCDs further transformed into a three-dimensional porous carbon frameworks, resembling a foam-like configuration to yield the intermediate product Co@NCFs. Finally, the three-dimensional porous foam-like CoTe2@NCFs catalyst was prepared at 700 °C under Ar atmosphere by the direct tellurisation of Co@NCFs. For comparison, sulfidation and selenisation were employed to obtain CoS2@NCFs and CoSe2@NCFs, respectively.

The microscopic morphologies of CoTe2@NCFs and other samples were characterised using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The carbonised Co@NCFs exhibited a three-dimensional porous foam-like carbon skeleton with rough surfaces (Fig. 2 b and Fig. 2 c). The crystalline phases of the as-prepared samples were revealed using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. S4, the diffraction peaks at 44.3°, 51.5°, and 75.9° could be ascribed to the (111), (200), and (220) facets of metallic Co (JCPDS 15–0806), respectively [46,47], indicating that metallic Co was successfully incorporated into the N-doped carbon frameworks (NCFs). After high-temperature tellurisation, the CoTe2@NCFs also exhibited a porous foam-like structure similar to that of the intermediate Co@NCFs (Fig. 2 d and Fig. 2 e), featuring a relatively smooth surface. Notably, upon sulfidation or selenisation, the morphologies of CoS2@NCFs and CoSe2@NCFs differed significantly from those of CoTe2@NCFs (Fig. S5). The porous foam structures underwent severe shrinkage, resulting in the formation of particles of varying sizes. The presence of fluffy N-rich carbon frameworks and interdigitated macropores may enhance active-site exposure and facilitate mass transport during catalysis [48]. The TEM images in Fig. 2 f reveal the dispersion of CoTe2 NPs with an average size of approximately 47.5 nm within the three-dimensional N-doped carbon frameworks, which effectively enhances the number of active sites. This dispersion can be attributed to the coordination between the N-rich functional groups in NCDs and Co2+ ions, preventing CoTe2 NPs from agglomerating during the high-temperature carbonisation and tellurisation processes. Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2 g) of CoTe2@NCFs shows two lattice fringe spacings of 0.28 and 0.34 nm, which can be assigned to the (110) crystal plane of CoTe2 NPs [49] and (002) plane of graphitic carbon [50], thereby confirming the successful assembly of CoTe2 with NCFs. The NCFs are consisted of multi-layer graphitic carbon with a thickness of 2–5 nm. The close integration between CoTe2 and NCFs can facilitate the generation of abundant active sites and accelerate the electrocatalytic reaction kinetics. Moreover, the lattice fringe spacings of 0.28 nm and 0.26 nm are associated with the (200) plane of CoS2 [51] and (210) plane of CoSe2 [52], respectively (Fig. S6). High-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) and the corresponding elemental mapping analyses showed uniformly dispersed Co, Te, C, and N on the CoTe2@NCFs (Fig. 2 h), further confirming the integrated architecture of CoTe2 and NCFs.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 i, the CoTe2@NCFs exhibit identical X-ray diffraction peaks at 26.9°, 31.8°, and 33.1°, which are well-indexed to the (110), (111), and (012) planes of the orthorhombic CoTe2 phase (JCPDS 74–0245) (Table S1), respectively [53,54], indicating the successful transformation of Co@NCFs into the corresponding telluride. In the case of CoS2@NCFs, the observed diffraction peaks can be attributed to the cubic CoS2 phase (JCPDS 41–1471) (Fig. S7a and Table S2) [55]. Moreover, the diffraction peaks of the CoSe2@NCFs are in good agreement with those of the cubic CoSe2 phase (JCPDS 09–0234) (Fig. S7b and Table S3) [22,56]. It is worth noting that the peak of graphitized carbon is not obviously shown, which may be attributed to the higher crystallinity of the diffraction peak of metal chalcogenides, the diffraction peak intensity of which is much higher than that of graphitized carbon. The wettability of the catalysts in air was investigated by measuring the contact angles. Fig. 2 j shows that the surface of the CoTe2@NCFs exhibits a significantly lower contact angle (11.3°) with KOH solution than the surfaces of CoS2@NCFs (40.6°) and CoSe2@NCFs (25.8°). It is attributed to the fact that CoTe2@NCFs has a lower surface roughness than CoS2@NCFs and CoSe2@NCFs. Benefiting from the three-dimensional porous foam-like structure and low surface roughness, the CoTe2@NCFs exhibited high hydrophilicity in air (Fig. 2 k and Fig. 2 l). These observations suggest that the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst exhibits superior wettability and hydrophilicity, facilitating efficient interactions between the active sites and the alkaline electrolyte and thus improving the catalytic kinetics of the ORR and OER.

In the Raman spectra (Fig. 3
a), two prominent peaks of the d-band (disordered carbon and structure defects) and G-band (graphitic sp2 carbon) were observed at 1343 and 1581 cm−1, respectively [57]. By Gaussian fitting, the I G/I D ratio of CoTe2@NCFs was determined to be 1.16, surpassing that of CoS2@NCFs (1.03) and CoSe2@NCFs (1.09), suggesting a higher degree of graphitisation and enhanced conductivity [58]. In addition, CoTe2@NCFs shows a 2D peak at about 2721 cm−1, implying that the graphene in CoTe2@NCFs are single or few layers, which is conducive to improving electrical conductivity and charge transfer. An isotherm analysis of CoTe2@NCFs revealed type-IV curves accompanied by a distinct hysteresis loop (Fig. 3 b), suggesting the existence of both microporous and mesoporous architectures [59]. According to the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method, the surface area of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst was determined to be 232.7 m2 g −1, which exceeds that of CoS2@NCFs (114.9 m2 g −1) and CoSe2@NCFs (201.1 m2 g −1), suggesting that tellurisation can further enhance the specific surface area of the catalyst. Furthermore, the CoTe2@NCFs exhibited a comparable nonuniformly distributed meso‑microlayered porous structure (Fig. S8). The hierarchical porous structure of CoTe2@NCFs is beneficial for exposing more active sites and enhancing the contact between the catalyst surface and electrolyte, thereby benefiting the catalytic process [8]. To evaluate the thermal stability and determine the carbon content of the CoS2@NCFs, CoSe2@NCFs, and CoTe2@NCFs catalysts, thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed under an O2 atmosphere. The initial weight losses of the CoS2@NCFs, CoSe2@NCFs, and CoTe2@NCFs were 17.6, 9.5, and 7.2 %, respectively (Fig. 3 c), which were primarily attributed to the desorption of water on the sample surface. The subsequent weight loss stages (70.3 %, 79.7 %, and 64.1 %) can be partly attributed to the conversion of metal chalcogenides into their respective oxides (Co3O4) and gaseous byproducts, such as SO2, SeO2, or TeO2, whereas the other portion is due to carbon oxidation, resulting in CO2 formation [60]. Co3O4 was identified as the final oxidation product at 900 °C (Fig. S9). According to the TG curves, the calculated carbon contents of CoS2@NCFs, CoSe2@NCFs, and CoTe2@NCFs were 66.3, 71.2, and 79.8 %, respectively. A higher carbon content can improve electrical conductivity, which is conducive to improving the intrinsic activity and stability of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the surface chemical compositions and valence states of the as-prepared catalysts. Fig. S10a shows the presence of (Co, S, N, and C), (Co, Se, N, and C), and (Co, Te, N, and C) in the CoS2@NCFs, CoSe2@NCFs, and CoTe2@NCFs catalysts, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 d, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 284.7 (CC), 286.1 (C–O/C–N), and 288.4 eV (C–O) [61]. The 2p3/2/2p1/2 doublet of the Co 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 e) can be divided into eight peaks: Co2+ (783.8 and 800.1 eV), Co3+ (781.5 and 797.6 eV), and Co–N (785.9 and 802.8 eV), as well as satellite peaks at 788.3 and 804.7 eV [62,63]. The two peaks located at 161.8 and 163.1 eV can be assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 (Fig. S10b). In addition, the peak at 168.2 eV is ascribed to the presence of S–O on the surface of CoSe2@NCFs [64]. Fig. S10c shows the Se 3d5/2 and Se 3d3/2 peaks located at 55.2 and 56.1 eV, respectively [65]. In addition, the peak at 59.5 eV is attributed to the formation of Se–O by selenium oxidation [66]. In the high-resolution Te 3d XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 3 f, the peaks positioned at 573.9 and 584.3 eV are assigned to Te 3d5/2 and Te 3d3/2, respectively [67]. The peaks at 576.4 and 586.8 eV are ascribed to Te–O, which suggests the presence of oxidised Te on the surface of CoTe2@NCFs [68]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectra of CoTe2@NCFs and other comparison samples (Fig. 3 g) can be deconvoluted into five peaks assigned to pyridine–N (397.5 eV), pyrrolic–N (399.7 eV), graphite–N (401.1 eV), Co–N (398.6 eV), and oxidised-N (402.9 eV) [69]. Compared to the CoS2@NCFs and CoSe2@NCFs catalysts, the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst contains higher amounts of pyridine–N, Co–N, and graphite–N (Fig. S10d and Table S4), indicating that the incorporation of N can effectively enhance the coupling between the metal center and the carbon matrix. Strong electron coupling can greatly increase the conductivity of the catalyst and the number of intrinsic active sites, thus improving the catalytic activity of the ORR and OER. Furthermore, the local coordination configuration and oxidation state of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst were then determined by X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS). Additionally, Co foil, Co2O3, CoPc, CoS2@NCFs, and CoSe2@NCFs were analyzed for comparison. The Co K-edge XANES spectrum of CoTe2@NCFs has a characteristic front edge peak at 7713 eV, which belongs to the 1s→3d electronic transition. In addition, charge transfer also occurred from the ligand to the metal (Fig. 3 h). Moreover, the intensity of the peak corresponding to the 1s→4pz transition in the spectrum of CoTe2@NCFs falls between that of Co foil and Co2O3, indicating strong interfacial interactions between the porous foam-like carbon frameworks and the CoTe2 NPs. Notably, the absorption threshold of the Co K-edge is located between those of the Co foil and Co2O3, and it can be inferred that the Co valence state in CoTe2@NCFs is between 0 and +3 [70,71]. Furthermore, as can be seen from the Co K-edge in the EXAFS spectrum in Fig. 3 i, the typical Co–S, Co–Se, and Co–Te bonds are represented by a reasonably significant peak at 1.86, 2.13, and 2.31 Å in CoS2@NCFs, CoSe2@NCFs, and CoTe2@NCFs, respectively [72,73]. Additionally, the distinct shoulder peak at 1.45 Å in the CoS2@NCFs, CoSe2@NCFs, and CoTe2@NCFs spectra can be attributed to the first shell coordination of Co–N [74]. Notably, the intensity of the Co–N peak in CoTe2@NCFs surpasses that observed in both CoS2@NCFs and CoSe2@NCFs, which is consistent with the XPS analysis results regarding the N content. Compared to Co foil and Co2O3, the EXAFS-wavelet transform (WT) maps of CoS2@NCFs, CoSe2@NCFs, and CoTe2@NCFs show maximum intensity contour peaks at approximately 5.5, 7.9, and 9.5 Å −1 (Fig. 3 j), corresponding to the Co–S, Co–Se, and Co–Te bonding, respectively. Additionally, a peak with a maximum intensity around 4.1 Å −1 was observed for the contour representing the Co–N coordination in CoTe2@NCFs. It is worth noting that no obvious Co–N peak can be found in CoS2@NCFs and CoSe2@NCFs, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 3 i. These findings provide additional evidence for the formation of NCoTe2 active sites in CoTe2@NCFs, which may work in concert to enhance both the electrical conductivity of the porous N-doped carbon frameworks and charge transport in CoTe2 NPs.

The ORR performances of the as-prepared catalysts and a commercial Pt/C catalyst were evaluated in an O2-saturated 0.1 mol L −1 KOH solution using a three-electrode system. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst exhibited a prominent oxygen reduction potential (0.79 V vs. RHE) comparable to that of Pt/C (Fig. S11), demonstrating a remarkable intrinsic ORR. As shown in Fig. 4
a and Fig. S12, according to the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves, the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst has a half-wave potential (E 1/2) of 0.85 V and onset potential (E onset) of 0.92 V, which is comparable to the commercial Pt/C catalyst (E 1/2 = 0.86 V, E onset = 0.93 V), but surpass those of NCFs (E 1/2 = 0.71 V, E onset = 0.76 V), CoS2@NCFs (E 1/2 = 0.79 V, E onset = 0.86 V), CoSe2@NCFs (E 1/2 = 0.82 V, E onset = 0.88 V), as well as other recently reported catalysts (Table S5). Moreover, the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst displayed a smaller Tafel slope of 59.5 mV dec−1 under alkaline conditions seen in Fig. 4 b, which was similar to that of the Pt/C catalyst (57.3 mV dec−1) but lower than that of NCFs (72.8 mV dec−1), CoS2@NCFs (68.1 mV dec−1), and CoSe2@NCFs (65.3 mV dec−1), suggesting that CoTe2@NCFs could significantly improve ORR dynamics. To investigate the electron transfer mechanism of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst during the ORR, the data for LSV curves were collected at speeds ranging from 400 to 1600 rpm (Fig. 4 c and Fig. S13). The observed increase in the limiting current density with increasing rotational speed suggested a reduction in the diffusion distance, indicating first-order dissolved oxygen saturation kinetics [75]. Based on the similar linearity of the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plot in the range of 0.25–0.55 V, the calculated electron transfer number (n) of CoTe2@NCFs was 3.92, which is higher than that of NCFs (3.73), CoS2@NCFs (3.81), and CoSe2@NCFs (3.87), implying that the electron transfer process on the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst is predominantly a four-electron transfer process. The n values and hydroperoxide yields (H2O2 %) were calculated from the rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) polarisation curves of the CoTe2@NCFs and Pt/C catalysts (Fig. S14). The corresponding H2O2 content was less than 8 %, and the calculated n was 3.9 in the 0.2–0.6 V potential range, which followed from the results of the K-L plot. The stability of the catalyst was evaluated by chronoamperometric and CV measurements (Fig. S15) as a critical criterion for practical application. The ORR current of the CoTe2@NCFs was maintained at 92.1 % for over 24000s, outperforming that of commercial Pt/C (75.9 %). Compared with commercial Pt/C, the polarisation curves of CoTe2@NCFs before and after 1000 cycles showed only a slight degradation in catalytic performance. To evaluate the catalyst stability, the morphology and composition of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst after the ORR stability test were characterised. The TEM and HRTEM images show that orthorhombic CoTe2 completely coated with NCFs even after the ORR durability test (Fig. S16a and Fig. S16b), indicating that the morphology of CoTe2@NCFs remained unaltered. In addition, the XPS spectra exhibiting unaltered binding energies for the Co, Te, C, and N peaks (Fig. S16c-f) indicated that the catalyst was nearly the same as the pristine CoTe2@NCFs catalyst. These results demonstrate the exceptional stability of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst towards ORR. The remarkable ORR activity was attributed to the fact that the active sites of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst were wrapped by a 3D foam-like N-doped carbon frameworks, which prevented rapid dissolution and corrosion of the active species, thereby improving the chemical stability of the catalyst [76].

Using a three-electrode setup, the electrocatalytic activity of various OER catalysts was performed in a 0.1 mol L −1 KOH electrolyte. The LSV curves in Fig. 4 d reveal that the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst requires a significantly lower overpotential of 280 mV for the OER to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2, outperforming that of NCFs (430 mV), CoS2@NCFs (360 mV), CoSe2@NCFs (310 mV), commercial RuO2 (330 mV), and other catalysts reported to date (Table S6). The Tafel slope of CoTe2@NCFs was measured to be 73.4 mV dec−1, which was lower than that of NCFs (88.2 mV dec−1), CoS2@NCFs (81.6 mV dec−1), CoSe2@NCFs (79.1 mV dec−1), and even RuO2 (74.9 mV dec−1), as depicted in Fig. 4 e. This indicates that the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst enables faster charge transfer at lower potentials at the electrocatalytic interface [77]. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the different samples was evaluated by measuring double-layer capacitances (C dl) in the range 10–100 mV s −1 by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 4 f and Fig. S17). As anticipated, the calculated C dl value of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst (19.7 mF cm−2) was significantly higher compared to the C dl values of NCFs (4.2 mF cm−2), CoS2@NCFs (8.9 mF cm−2), and CoSe2@NCFs (13.8 mF cm−2). These results unequivocally demonstrate that the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst possesses a large electrochemically active surface area, which provides abundant active sites for the OER. This enhanced ECSA can be attributed to the foam-like N-doped carbon frameworks, which facilitates facile access to active sites and ensures extensive electrode/electrolyte contact [78]. Furthermore, the exceptional catalytic activity of CoTe2@NCFs primarily stems from the elevated intrinsic conductivity of the CoTe2 NPs, which enables more efficient charge migration [13]. The charge-transfer kinetics of the catalysts were investigated using electrochemical impedance measurements. Among the tested catalysts, the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst displayed the smallest semicircle diameter in the high-frequency range (Fig. S18 and Table S7). The R ct value of CoTe2@NCFs (31.55 Ω) was significantly lower than that of NCFs (94.93 Ω), CoS2@NCFs (58.54 Ω), and CoSe2@NCFs (40.38 Ω), indicating the enhanced charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface. This phenomenon provides an explanation for the superior catalytic performance of CoTe2@NCFs. Furthermore, the durability of the CoTe2@NCFs for the OER was tested by chronoamperometric and CV measurements (Fig. S19). After 24000s of continuous reaction, the OER current of the CoTe2@NCFs retained 93.1 % of the initial current, surpassing that of commercial RuO2 (83.2 %). Additionally, the polarisation curves of the CoTe2@NCFs before and after 1000 cycles indicate only a negligible loss in catalytic performance compared to that of RuO2. After the durability test of the OER, the TEM image, HRTEM image, and XPS spectra (Fig. S20) confirmed that the morphology or chemical composition of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst after the stability test was altered only minimally compared to its original state. The extraordinary stability of CoTe2@NCFs during the ORR and OER can be ascribed to the safeguard offered by the highly conductive NCFs. In order to determine the effect of pyrolysis temperature on ORR/OER activity, electrochemical tests were performed on the catalysts prepared at varying pyrolysis temperatures (Fig. S21). It can be found that CoTe2@NCFs displayed the most distinguished performance in comparison to CoTe2@NCFs-800 (E 1/2 = 0.73 V, E onset = 0.82 V) and CoTe2@NCFs-1000 (E 1/2 = 0.78 V, E onset = 0.87 V). Furthermore, CoTe2@NCFs exhibited the lowest overpotential than that of CoTe2@NCFs-800 (410 mV) and CoTe2@NCFs-1000 (360 mV). As shown in Fig. S22, the loading amount of CoTe2 also affects the catalytic activity. When the CoTe2 loading is too low, the number of active sites decreases, resulting in reduced ORR and OER activities. Similarly, when the CoTe2 loading is too high, the particle surface area decreases and the interaction between particles increases, resulting in reduced ORR/OER catalytic activity.

In situ Raman spectroscopy coupled with electrochemistry was employed to validate the evolution of active substances and surface structures during the OER process of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst in a KOH electrolyte. Fig. S23 shows a schematic of the device used for in situ Raman testing in the field. As shown in Fig. 4 g and Fig. 4 h, lattice vibrations occurred in the spectral band between 200 and 900 cm−1. When the electrode was submerged in a KOH solution, no significant Raman peaks appeared under open-circuit conditions. Upon increasing the voltage from 1.45 V to 1.81 V, a peak that resembled the A2u vibration peak of β-Co(OH)2 began to emerge at 461 cm−1, indicating that the intermediate phase β-Co(OH)2 was produced during the OER process [79]. With further progression of the reaction, an A1g peak also appeared with increasing intensity at 575 cm−1, which can be ascribed to the production of the CoOOH intermediate [80]. These findings suggest that under harsh oxidative conditions, some active sites in CoTe2@NCFs are converted into β-Co(OH)2 active components, which may enhance the OER performance and protect the β-Co(OH)2 catalysts from corrosion [13]. The deviation of the OER potential at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 (E j=10) and the E 1/2 value for the ORR were determined to estimate the bifunctional oxygen catalytic activities of the catalyst (ΔE = E j=10 − E 1/2) [71]. In Fig. 4 i and Fig. S24, the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst presents a ∆E value of 0.66 V, which is much better than that for NCFs (0.95 V), CoS2@NCFs (0.80 V), CoSe2@NCFs (0.72 V), Pt/C (1.03 V), RuO2 (0.98 V), and several previously reported bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts (Table S8). These findings highlight the promising potential application of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst in rechargeable ZABs.

To obtain insight into the ORR/OER mechanism, three theoretical models for NCoS2, NCoSe2, and NCoTe2 were created using DFT calculations, as depicted in Fig. 5
a and Fig. S25. Four foundational active species, *OO, *OOH, *O, and *OH (* denotes an active site), are involved in both the ORR and OER [25,81]. The O2 adsorption energy of the NCoTe2 active site was determined to be −1.215 eV (Fig. 5 b), which is more negative than those of NCoS2 (−0.782 eV) and NCoSe2 (−0.947 eV), thus stabilizing the adsorption of O2 and promoting electron transfer. The computed free-energy plots of NCoS2, NCoSe2, and NCoTe2 for the ORR are shown in Fig. 5 c and Fig. 5 d. The free energies of the three models at U = 0 V indicated a thermodynamically spontaneous process (Fig. 5 c). The free energy changes for NCoS2, NCoSe2, and NCoTe2 were determined to be 0.61, 0.69, and 0.81 eV, respectively, indicating that the electrocatalytic activity for the ORR is higher at the active site of NCoTe2. As shown in Fig. 5 d, the last elementary step (protonation of *OH to H2O) in NCoTe2 and NCoSe2 was the limiting step. At the standard potential of U = 1.23 V, the corresponding Gibbs free energy changes for NCoTe2 and NCoSe2 were 0.42 eV and 0.54 eV, respectively. In contrast, in the case of NCoS2, the first elementary step, the reduction of O2 to *OOH, had the highest free energy difference of up to 0.62 eV, followed by an additional energy barrier of 0.22 eV. In addition, the activities of NCoTe2 and NCoSe2 were primarily determined by the adsorption-free energy of *OH (ΔG *OH), with NCoTe2 exhibiting a ΔG *OH value of 0.81 eV, surpassing that of NCoSe2 (ΔG *OH = 0.69 eV). Compared with NCoTe2 and NCoSe2, the d-band center of NCoS2 moves up significantly, which is not conducive to the adsorption of oxygen at the active site, resulting in a significant increase in the energy barrier. DFT calculations were performed to gain insight into the OER performances of NCoS2, NCoSe2, and NCoTe2 (Fig. 5 e and Fig. 5 f). At U = 0 V, the rate-limiting step for all three models was the deprotonation of *OH, in contrast to that observed for the ORR process. Notably, for the NCoTe2 system, the energy barrier for this limiting step was as low as 1.53 eV (Fig. 5 g), which is slightly lower than the energy barriers of NCoS2 (1.84 eV) and NCoSe2 (1.61 eV). At U = 1.23 V, the rate-determining step was also the deprotonation of *OH, and NCoS2, NCoSe2, and NCoTe2 exhibited energy barriers of 0.61, 0.38, and 0.30 eV respectively. This suggests that the incorporation of the NCoTe2 active site in the electrocatalyst significantly reduces the energy barrier and enhances the intrinsic OER activity. Therefore, the NCoTe2 active site demonstrates superior bifunctional oxygen electrocatalytic activity for the ORR and OER due to its ability to lower the d/p band center during the ORR, which facilitates the protonation of *OH into H2O and accelerates *OH deprotonation during the OER [82].

The relationship between the energy gap at the d/p band center and the theoretical potential of the ORR and OER processes of NCoS2, NCoSe2, and NCoTe2 is shown in Fig. 5 h. For the ORR process, a smaller energy band difference (Δε d-p) leads to a higher reaction voltage and improved performance. Conversely, for the OER process, a smaller Δε d-p results in lower overpotential and increased intrinsic catalytic activity, which aligns with experimental observations. This finding underscores the crucial role played by the energy gap between the d-band and p-band in the ORR and OER. In alkaline media, both ORR and OER reactions at the NCoTe2 active site occur through four electronic pathways (Fig. 5 i). In the ORR process, the first step is the hydrogenation of O2 at the NCoTe2 active site to produce *OOH, followed by the formation of two substances *O and *OH, and the last step is the formation of OH−. The process of OER is the opposite of ORR, with the first step being the formation of *OH at the NCoTe2 active site, followed by the formation of *O and *OOH, and the final step being the formation of O2 and H2O molecules. The intermediates (*OO, *OOH, *O, and *OH) involved in the ORR/OER are nucleophilic [83].

In view of their excellent bifunctional ORR/OER characteristics, CoTe2@NCFs were assembled as rechargeable AZABs to demonstrate their potential applications. The homemade AZAB was fabricated by employing the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst as the air cathode and a Zn plate as the anode (Fig. 6
a and Fig. S26). Similarly, an AZAB composed of commercial Pt/C and RuO2 mixed materials (Pt/C+RuO2, with a weight ratio of 1:1) was also prepared for comparison. The CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB demonstrates a steady open-circuit voltage of 1.465 V (Fig. 6 b), which is superior to that of the Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB (1.433 V), indicating a lower overpotential and improved stability. The CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB exhibits a smaller voltage gap between charge and recharge than the Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB under identical conditions (Fig. 6 c), which is due to the better ORR/OER activities of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst. For instance, the gap of the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 was 1.31 V, which is smaller than that of the Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB (1.66 V). Remarkably, the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB can deliver a peak power density of 177.8 mW cm−2 at 298.4 mA cm−2 (Fig. 6 d), exceeding that of Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB (91.2 mW cm−2 at 159.7 mA cm−2). The CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB showed an impressive specific discharge capacity of 793.4 mAh g −1 Zn at 10 mA cm−2, which corresponds to a high energy density of 990.6 Wh kg−1 Zn (Fig. 6 e and Fig. S27). These values are superior to the respective values obtained for the Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB (686.8 mAh g −1 Zn corresponding to 804.1 Wh kg−1 Zn). Furthermore, when two CoTe2@NCFs-based AZABs are connected in series as a power source, a light-emitting diode (LED) screen marked with "CSU" can be successfully lit, confirming its application prospects in actual energy devices. As shown in Fig. 6 f, galvanostatic discharge tests were performed to assess recovery capability performance at current densities of 1–25 mA cm−2. The discharge potential plateau decreases and stabilises as the current density increases. The discharge voltage of the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB remained greater than that of the Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB in the range of 1–25 mA cm−2. The discharge voltage of the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB reverted to its initial value, demonstrating excellent lower polarisation and outstanding rate performance when the current density was restored to 1 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the CoTe2@NCFs- and Pt/C+RuO2-based AZABs were separately assembled and subjected to charging and discharging cycles at 10 mA cm−2 to further assess the cycling stability. Fig. S28 shows the impedance distribution of CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB and comparison samples. The intersection of this Nyquist plot with the x-axis is the battery's resistance. It can be found from the enlarged plot that the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB has the lowest resistance (close to 1.7 Ω), which lays the foundation for the performance under high current.

As illustrated in Fig. 6 g, the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB exhibited negligible voltage degradation between charge and discharge, even after 1000 cycles of continuous cycling, surpassing the behaviour of the Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB (320 cycles). Specifically, the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB demonstrated a minimal charge-discharge voltage gap of 0.68 V with an impressive round-trip efficiency of 65.1 % during the 5th cycle. The charge-discharge voltage gap remained steady at 0.76 V after 500 cycles of operation, while maintaining a round-trip cycle efficiency of 61.4 %. Even after 1000 cycles, only a slight reduction in the voltage gap to 0.81 V was observed, along with a marginal decrease in the round-trip cycle efficiency to 59.2 %, thus confirming the high efficiency and exceptional durability of the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB. The radar plot in Fig. 6 h effectively illustrates the superior properties of the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB with respect to the open-circuit voltage, power density, specific capacity, energy density, and cyclic stability compared to the Pt/C+RuO2-based AZAB. Compared with other reported rechargeable AZABs utilising similar catalysts, the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB exhibits remarkable efficiency and extended cycling life (Table S9). The excellent performance of the CoTe2@NCFs-based AZAB is attributed to the formation of a rich solid-liquid-gas three-phase reaction interface, which ensures that the intrinsic active site of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst is fully exposed to the alkaline electrolyte and accelerates the diffusion of reaction molecules or ions at the interface [50,84].

A three-dimensional foam-like CoTe2@NCFs catalyst with abundant NCoTe2 active sites was successfully constructed as an efficient bifunctional ORR/OER catalyst through CD-assisted strategy. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the incorporation of Te from the p-block effectively narrows the gap between the d-band center of Co and the p-band center of Te, thereby significantly enhancing the proximity of their d/p band center and promoting the balanced adsorption/desorption of oxygen-containing intermediates (*OH and *OOH), leading to improved ORR/OER performance. In situ Raman spectroscopy monitored the formation of Co-OOH on the surface of the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst, confirming the dynamic changes in the oxygen intermediates for enhancing the OER activity. The CoTe2@NCFs catalyst demonstrates exceptional bifunctional catalytic performance towards the ORR/OER with a ΔE of 0.66 V under alkaline conditions. Furthermore, a rechargeable AZAB assembled with the CoTe2@NCFs catalyst exhibits an open circuit voltage of 1.465 V, peak power density of 177.8 mW cm−2, specific capacity of 793.4 mAh g −1, high energy density of 990.6 Wh kg−1 Zn, and outstanding cycling stability over 1000 cycles at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The cooperative control of the d/p band center provides novel insights into the design of efficient bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts for energy storage and conversion devices.
